title: Vaccinium minus
intro: Vaccinium minus, also known as small cranberry or round-leaf cranberry, is a low-growing, evergreen shrub native to eastern North America. The health benefits of Vaccinium minus are attributed to its high content of vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants, which have been shown to prevent urinary tract infections, support heart health, and exhibit anti-inflammatory and anti-cancer properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include anthocyanins, flavonoids, phenolic acids, and proanthocyanidins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Vaccinium minus is commonly used to make herbal preparations such as teas, capsules, and extracts, which are used to support urinary tract health, prevent kidney stones, and reduce inflammation.
taxonomy: Vaccinium minus is classified as a species within the genus Vaccinium, which is a group of flowering plants in the family Ericaceae. This classification is part of the larger taxonomic hierarchy, placing Vaccinium minus as a member of the order Ericales. Within the family Ericaceae, Vaccinium minus is grouped alongside other similar species, such as blueberries and cranberries. The genus Vaccinium contains approximately 450 species, with Vaccinium minus being one of the lesser-known varieties. By classifying Vaccinium minus within the Ericaceae family, scientists can better understand its evolutionary history and relationships with other plants.
uses: Vaccinium minus, also known as small cranberry or small bilberry, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for various purposes. The plant's leaves and berries are rich in antioxidants and have been used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and stomach ulcers. Vaccinium minus has also been used to reduce inflammation and improve wound healing. Its leaves have been employed in traditional medicine to treat fever and colds. More research is needed to fully understand the medicinal potential of Vaccinium minus.
